---
status: accepted
---

# The host age key is generated by hand, and there is no sshd

sops-nix decrypts secrets at activation using the machine's **SSH host key**
(`/etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key`), which it converts to an age identity internally — so
the host needs no separate age keyfile to manage, back up, or leak. Two decisions follow
from wanting that key, and this ADR records both because the config looks under-configured
without them.

## No sshd

`services.openssh.enable = true` is the usual way a host key comes to exist, and we
deliberately do not use it. Enabling it would run a **listening daemon on a single-user
laptop purely as a side effect of wanting a key file** — a remote attack surface bolted
onto a config whose whole posture (ADR-0003, ADR-0005) is about minimising physical and
local exposure. We want the KEY, not the SERVER.

Consequence: `sops.age.sshKeyPaths` must be set **explicitly** in
`hosts/framework/secrets.nix`, because the module default derives it from
`services.openssh.enable`.

## Generated by hand in the installer, not by an activation script

Contrast `home.nix`, which *does* generate the commit signing key from an activation
script. That approach cannot work here, and the reason is ordering, not taste:

> A secret can only be decrypted by a key that was already a **recipient at encryption
> time**. So the host key must exist — and `.sops.yaml` must already list it — **before the
> first activation that reads a secret**.

A key generated mid-activation is already too late to decrypt anything, while making the
config *look* self-bootstrapping when it isn't. The chicken-and-egg is resolved by hand,
once, in the installer (`docs/hardware-acceptance.md`, Phase B).

## Considered Options

- **Enable sshd just to get the host key** — rejected. Buys a key at the cost of a network
  daemon nothing else wants; directly contradicts the minimal-exposure posture.
- **Generate the host key from an activation script** — rejected. Cannot work: the key
  would not have been a recipient when the committed ciphertext was encrypted, so the
  first activation still fails to decrypt.
- **A standalone age keyfile instead of reusing the SSH host key** — rejected. A second
  key to manage, back up, and leak, for no gain; sops-nix already speaks SSH host keys.

## Consequences

- **The host age key sits outside declarative management.** Nothing in the repo recreates
  it, and losing it means activation fails until the secrets are re-keyed. This is the same
  bargain the sbctl PKI bundle makes (ADR-0002) and is the price of not running a daemon we
  do not want. Losing the *host* key is a re-key; losing the *admin* key is a loss
  (CONTEXT.md).
- **A reinstall requires a re-key** before the machine can decrypt anything, so
  `.sops.yaml` and `sops updatekeys` are part of every install, not one-time setup. The
  recipient-count guard in `hosts/framework/secrets.nix` is what catches a forgotten
  `updatekeys` at build time rather than at first boot.
